🌡️ Micro:bit Project Blog

Thermometer

A servo-powered temperature gauge using the Micro:bit and Sensor:bit board

By: Adrien B

Difficulty: Intermediate
Language: TypeScript — MakeCode
Hardware: Micro:bit + Sensor:bit
Servo: 180° Servo Motor (P1)
Extension: Servos

What Is It?

The Thermometer uses the Micro:bit's built-in temperature sensor to drive a physical 180° servo motor — turning it into a real analogue dial thermometer. The hotter it gets, the further the servo arm rotates. The current temperature also scrolls across the LED display at all times.

It uses the ELECFREAKS Sensor:bit IO Breakout Board, which makes it easy to connect a servo motor to the Micro:bit's pins without extra wiring.

Servo angle range

❄️ 0°
✅ 0° → 180°
🔥 180°
Below 10°C — locked at 0° 10°C – 40°C — proportional Above 40°C — locked at 180°

Hardware You Need

PartDetails
🖥️ BBC Micro:bitAny version — has a built-in temperature sensor
🔌 ELECFREAKS Sensor:bitIO breakout board — exposes all Micro:bit pins with easy connectors
⚙️ 180° Servo MotorConnected to pin P1 on the Sensor:bit
🔋 Power supplyUSB or battery pack to power the Sensor:bit and servo
⚠️ Important: You must add the Servos extension in MakeCode before this code will work. Go to Extensions → search "Servos" → click to add it.

How It Works

1
On start — servo resets to 0° When the Micro:bit powers on, the servo immediately moves to 0° so the dial always starts from the same position.
2
Temperature is read in a loop The forever loop keeps reading the temperature and updating the servo angle and LED display continuously.
3
Servo angle is calculated from temperature Between 10°C and 40°C, the servo angle is calculated using a formula: (temperature − 10) × 6. This maps 30 degrees of temperature to 180 degrees of servo rotation.
4
Edge cases are clamped If the temperature is above 40°C the servo locks at 180°. Below 10°C it locks at 0°. This stops the servo from being sent out-of-range values.
The angle formula (for temperatures between 10°C and 40°C):
servo angle = (temperature − 10) × 6
Example: 25°C → (25 − 10) × 6 = 15 × 6 = 90° (halfway)
Example: 10°C → (10 − 10) × 6 = 0 × 6 = 0° (minimum)
Example: 40°C → (40 − 10) × 6 = 30 × 6 = 180° (maximum)

Block 1 — On Start

This block runs once when the Micro:bit powers on. It resets the servo to 0° so the dial always starts from the cold end.

  • set servo P1 angle to 0° — sends a 0° signal to the servo connected to pin P1 on the Sensor:bit. This is from the Servos extension, which must be added in MakeCode first.

Block 2 — Forever (Main Loop)

This is the brain of the thermometer. It runs in a continuous loop, reading the temperature and deciding what angle to set the servo to, then displaying the temperature on the LEDs.

What each part does:

  • forever — this loop repeats endlessly, keeping the servo and display always up to date with the latest temperature reading.
  • if temperature ≤ 40 and temperature ≥ 10 then — the main range check. Both conditions must be true at the same time for this branch to run. This is the normal operating range.
  • set servo P1 angle to (temperature − 10) × 6 — the formula that converts temperature to servo angle. Subtracting 10 starts the range at 0, then multiplying by 6 spreads 30°C across 180° of rotation.
  • else if temperature > 40 then → set angle to 180° — if it's dangerously hot, the servo locks at the maximum position so it doesn't receive an out-of-range command.
  • else if temperature < 10 then → set angle to 0° — if it's very cold, the servo locks at the minimum position for the same reason.
  • show number temperature (°C) — after the servo is updated, the current temperature scrolls across the LED display so you can also read the exact number.

The Completed Code (Blocks)

Here are both blocks together — the full thermometer program:

Block 1 runs once to initialise the servo. Block 2 then runs forever, continuously reading the temperature and keeping the servo and display in sync.

Key Coding Ideas Used

  • Analogue output via servo — instead of just showing data on a screen, this project uses a physical servo motor to represent data. The angle of the arm is the "reading" — just like a real dial thermometer.
  • Linear mapping — the formula (temp − 10) × 6 maps one range (10–40°C) to another (0–180°). This technique is used everywhere in electronics and data visualisation.
  • Clamping — the else if branches "clamp" the servo to safe values (0° or 180°) when the input goes outside the expected range. This protects the hardware.
  • Compound if conditions — using && (and) to check two conditions at once keeps the code shorter and easier to read than writing two separate if statements.
  • Forever loop — running the logic continuously means the thermometer always reflects the current temperature, not just a one-time reading.
  • Servos extension — this project uses an extra MakeCode extension beyond the built-in blocks, showing how to expand what the Micro:bit can do.

Try It Yourself — Run the Code Online

Open the MakeCode editor, add the Servos extension, then paste the JavaScript below.

🌐 MakeCode Micro:bit Editor
Open makecode.microbit.org ↗ Extensions → search "Servos" → add it → paste JavaScript → switch to Blocks view

The Code in JavaScript

Copy and paste this into MakeCode's JavaScript tab — it will automatically convert to blocks!

thermometer.js 
servos.P1.setAngle(0)

basic.forever(function () {
    if (input.temperature() <= 40 && input.temperature() >= 10) {
        servos.P1.setAngle((input.temperature() - 10) * 6)
    } else if (input.temperature() > 40) {
        servos.P1.setAngle(180)
    } else if (input.temperature() < 10) {
        servos.P1.setAngle(0)
    }
    basic.showNumber(input.temperature())
})
💡 Tip: Remember to add the Servos extension first — without it, MakeCode won't recognise the servos.P1.setAngle() commands.

Ideas to Improve It

Add colour-coded LED icons — show a snowflake below 10°C, a sun above 30°C, and a flame above 40°C.
Draw tick marks on a printed scale card that sits behind the servo arm to make it look like a real dial.
Log the temperature over time using radio to send readings to a second Micro:bit.
Add a buzzer alarm that sounds when the temperature goes above a set threshold.
Try using an external temperature sensor for more accurate readings than the Micro:bit's built-in one.
